notes: First major draft for words_language — 1 active clue at time of authoring (Esperanto). 24 Wikipedia lead-extract docs built via build-topic-docs.mjs into data/sourcing/docs/words_language/.
Subcategory spread: Foreign Words & Phrases (7), Etymology (3), Definitions (5).
All clues are grounded only in facts from the cited source docs. Difficulty: $200 = direct translation/definition; $400 = requires knowing the concept or context; $600 = oblique/specialist hook.

questions.clue: This Latin aphorism, translated as 'seize the day,' appears in book 1 of the Roman poet Horace's Odes, composed around 23 BC.

citations.title: Carpe diem
citations.snippet: Carpe diem is a Latin aphorism, usually translated 'seize the day', taken from book 1 of the Roman poet Horace's work Odes.

questions.clue: This English word for a food of filling placed between two slices of bread takes its name from John Montagu, an 18th-century British nobleman so devoted to gaming that he reputedly ate his meal without leaving the card table.

citations.title: Sandwich
citations.snippet: A sandwich is a dish typically consisting variously of meat, cheese, sauces, and vegetables used as a filling between slices of bread, or placed atop a slice of bread ; or, more generally, any dish in which bread serves as a container or wr

questions.clue: The words 'madam' and 'racecar,' and the sentence 'A man, a plan, a canal — Panama,' are classic examples of this term for a sequence that reads identically forwards and backwards.

citations.title: Palindrome
citations.snippet: A palindrome is a word, number, phrase, or other sequence of symbols that reads the same backwards as forwards, such as madam or racecar, the date "22/02/2022", or the sentence "A man, a plan, a canal – Panama". The 19-letter Finnish word s

questions.clue: The substitution of an inoffensive or mild expression for one that might be considered unpleasant — often used when discussing death, disability, sex, or violence — defines this common rhetorical device.

citations.title: Euphemism
citations.snippet: A euphemism is the substitution of a potentially offensive or unpleasant word or expression with one that is more pleasant or inoffensive. Some euphemisms are humorous, while others use mild or neutral language to downplay certain concepts.

questions.clue: This German loanword, literally translating as 'harm-joy,' names the pleasure derived from learning of another's troubles, failures, or humiliation.

citations.title: Schadenfreude
citations.snippet: Schadenfreude is the experience of pleasure, joy, or self-satisfaction that comes from learning of the troubles, failures, pain, suffering, or humiliation of another. Schadenfreude is a loanword from German. The closest word in English woul

questions.clue: In folklore and fiction, this German-origin loanword names a supernatural twin of a living person, perceived as a harbinger of bad luck.

citations.title: Doppelgänger
citations.snippet: A doppelgänger is a supernatural double of a living person, especially one who haunts the doubled person. In fiction and mythology and in common parlance, the doppelgänger either is a ghost or a paranormal phenomenon, usually perceived as t

questions.clue: French for 'already seen,' this phrase names the eerie phenomenon of feeling that a present moment has been experienced before — an illusion of memory with no verifiable prior context.

citations.title: Déjà vu
citations.snippet: Déjà vu is the phenomenon of feeling like one has lived through the present situation in the past. It is an illusion of memory whereby—despite a strong sense of recollection—the time, place, and context of the "previous" experience are unce

questions.clue: This Latin legal maxim translates as 'let the buyer beware' and holds that the purchaser — having less information than the seller — should examine goods before buying.

citations.title: Caveat emptor
citations.snippet: Caveat emptor is Latin for "Let the buyer beware". It has become a proverb in English. Generally, caveat emptor is the contract law principle that controls the sale of real property after the date of closing, but may also apply to sales of

questions.clue: This English word for organized non-participation entered the language in 1880, named after a County Mayo land agent in Ireland who was subjected to the tactic at the suggestion of nationalist leader Charles Stewart Parnell.

citations.title: Boycott
citations.snippet: A boycott is an act of nonviolent, voluntary abstention from a product, person, organization, or country as an expression of protest. It is usually for moral, social, political, or environmental reasons. The purpose of a boycott is to infli

questions.clue: Derived from the Greek words for 'sharp' and 'dull,' this rhetorical term for a self-contradictory phrase — such as 'bittersweet' or 'cruel kindness' — is itself an example of what it names.

citations.title: Oxymoron
citations.snippet: An oxymoron is a figure of speech that juxtaposes concepts with opposite meanings within a word or in a phrase that is a self-contradiction. Examples would be "bittersweet" or "cruel kindness". As a rhetorical device, an oxymoron illustrate

questions.clue: Words like 'oink,' 'meow,' 'roar,' and 'chirp' — as well as 'beep,' 'simmer,' and 'hiccup' — are all examples of this rhetorical term for a word that phonetically imitates the sound it represents.

citations.title: Onomatopoeia
citations.snippet: Onomatopoeia is a type of word, or the process of creating a word, that phonetically imitates, resembles, or suggests a sound that it refers to. Common onomatopoeias in English include animal noises such as oink, meow, roar, and chirp, amon

questions.clue: When Yogi Berra reportedly called a switch-hitter 'amphibious' instead of 'ambidextrous,' he illustrated this term for the unintentional substitution of one word for another with a similar sound.

citations.title: Malapropism
citations.snippet: A malapropism is the incorrect use of a word in place of a word with a similar sound, either unintentionally or for comedic effect, resulting in a nonsensical, often humorous utterance. An example is the statement attributed to baseball pla

questions.clue: Originally the name of a Romance-based pidgin used by traders across the Mediterranean Basin from the 11th to the 19th centuries, this Italian-origin phrase now refers to any language used as a common medium between groups with different native tongues.

citations.title: Lingua franca
citations.snippet: A lingua franca, also known as a bridge language, common language, trade language, auxiliary language, link language, or language of wider communication (LWC), is a language systematically used to make communication possible between groups

questions.clue: This German philosophical term, literally meaning 'spirit of the age,' describes the dominant intellectual and cultural climate of a given historical epoch and is especially associated with the philosophy of Hegel.

citations.title: Zeitgeist
citations.snippet: In 18th- and 19th-century German philosophy, a Zeitgeist is a way of referring to the intellectual, cultural, ethical and political climate of a given epoch in world history. It is often described as an invisible agent, force, or daemon tha

questions.clue: Lewis Carroll coined this linguistic term in Through the Looking-Glass (1871) to describe a word blended from two others — as in 'smog' from 'smoke' and 'fog,' or 'motel' from 'motor' and 'hotel.'

citations.title: Portmanteau
citations.snippet: In literature, a portmanteau, also known in linguistics and lexicography as a blend word, lexical blend, or simply a blend, is a word formed by combining the meanings and parts of the sounds of two or more words. English examples include sm
